## Vladimir D. Tonchev

### Michigan Technological University

## Coding Theory, Combinatorial Designs, and Finite Geometry

Error-correcting codes are used to protect data from random errors in satellite and wireless communication systems, audio and video recording devices, and data storage devices. A large class of codes with a wide range of applications are based on finite geometry. The most notable example of such codes are the Reed-Muller codes that are being used in deep space and mobile communications.

The subject of this talk is a class of codes based on combinatorial designs. These combinatorial codes possess remarkable error-correction properties, admit efficient decoding, and present a viable alternative to a subclass of Reed-Muller codes.

Тhe lecture will be held in Hall 403, Institute of Mathematics and Informatics at the Bulgarian Academy of Sciences.

Combinatorial design[](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/code_graph#code_combinatorial_design)
===========================================================================================

Alternative names: Block design, Covering design.

Description
-----------

A constant-weight binary code that is mapped into a combinatorial t-design.

The mapping proceeds as follows for a length-n code with codewords of constant weight w. A codeword c corresponds to a block of the design, with the codeword's j th coordinate labeling whether or not element j is contained in the block. There are a total of n elements, and the constant weight of the code implies that each block contains a fixed number w of elements. The code supports an S(t,w,n)Steiner system if each subset of t≤w elements is contained in exactly one block. More generally, the code supports a combinatorial t-design, or, more precisely, a t-(n,w,λ)-design, if each such t-subset is contained in exactly λ≥1 blocks. A combinatorial 2-design with two block intersection sizes is called a quasi-symmetric design. A 3-(q d+1,q+1,1) combinatorial design is sometimes called a Witt design (a.k.a. a spherical geometry design) [[1](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-1),[3](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-3)][[2; Remark 6.10](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-2)].

For example, the eight codewords of weight w=3 of the [7,4,3] Hamming code support a 2-(7,3,1)-design a.k.a. an S(2,3,7) Steiner system. The codeword 1000110 corresponds to a block containing elements 1, 5, and 6. Similarly, the other seven codewords correspond to blocks 257, 367, 147, 246, 345, and 123. Each pair of elements is contained in exactly one block.

Combinatorial t-designs exist for all t[[4](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-4)–[8](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-8)]. Existence of certain quasi-symmetric designs has also been established [[9]](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-9).

Notes
-----

See [[10](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-10)–[12](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-12)] for reviews on combinatorial designs.See [[2](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-2),[13](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-13)–[15](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-15)] for books on combinatorial designs.

Cousins
-------

*   [q-ary code](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/q-ary_digits_into_q-ary_digits)—Designs can be constructed from q-ary codes by taking the supports of a subset of codewords of constant weight.
*   [Reed-Muller (RM) code](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/reed_muller)—Fixed-weight RM codewords of weight less than 2 m support combinatorial 3-designs [[11; Exam. 5.2.7]](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-11).
*   [[7,4,3] Hamming code](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/hamming743)—Weight-three and weight-four codewords of the [7,4,3] Hamming code support combinatorial 2-(7,3,1) and 2-(7,4,2) designs, respectively [[11; Exam. 5.2.5]](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-11).
*   [[2 r−1,2 r−r−1,3] Hamming code](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/hamming)—Weight-three codewords of the [2 r−1,2 r−r−1,3] Hamming code support the Steiner system S(2,3,2 r−1)[[16; pg. 89]](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-16).
*   [[2 r,2 r−r−1,4] Extended Hamming code](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/extended_hamming)—Weight-four codewords of the [2 r,2 r−r−1,4] extended Hamming code support the Steiner system S(3,4,2 r)[[16; pg. 89]](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-16).
*   [Quadratic-residue (QR) code](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/q-ary_quad_residue)—The supports of fixed-weight codewords of certain q-ary QR codes support combinatorial designs [[12](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-12),[17](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-17),[18](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-18)].
*   [Pless symmetry code](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/pless_symmetry)—The supports of fixed-weight codewords of certain Pless symmetry codes support combinatorial designs [[12](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-12),[19](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-19),[20](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-20)].
*   [[23,12,7] Golay code](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/golay)—The supports of the weight-seven codewords of the Golay code support the Steiner system S(4,7,23)[[12](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-12),[21](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-21)][[16; pg. 89](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-16)]. Its blocks are called octads.
*   [[24,12,8] Extended Golay code](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/extended_golay)—The supports of the weight-eight codewords of the extended Golay code support the Steiner system S(5,6,12)[[12](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-12),[21](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-21)][[16; pg. 89](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-16)]. Its blocks are called octads.
*   [[11,6,5]3 Ternary Golay code](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/ternary_golay)—The supports of the weight-five (weight-six) codewords of the (extended) ternary Golay code support the Steiner system S(4,5,11) (S(5,6,12)) [[12](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-12),[21](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-21)][[16; pg. 89](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-16)]. Its blocks are called hexads.
*   [Perfect code](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/perfect)—Perfect codes and combinatorial designs are related [[17](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-17),[22](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-22)].
*   [Dual linear code](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/dual)—Linear codes and their duals are related to combinatorial designs via the Assmus-Mattson theorem [[18](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-18),[23](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-23)] (see [[11; Sec. 5.4]](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-11)).
*   [Self-dual linear code](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/self_dual)—Self-dual extremal codes yield combinatorial ≤5-designs using the Assmus-Mattson theorem [[18]](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-18) (see [[11; Sec. 5.4]](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-11)). See [[24; Table 1.61, pg. 683]](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-24) for a table of combinatorial designs obtained from self-dual codes.
*   [Gallager (GL) code](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/gallager)—Some Steiner systems can be used to construct Gallager codes [[25]](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-25).
*   [Algebraic LDPC code](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/algebraic_ldpc)—Combinatorial designs can be used to construct explicit LDPC codes [[26](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-26)–[28](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-28)].
*   [Hadamard code](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/hadamard)—Hadamard designs are combinatorial designs constructed from Hadamard matrices [[29]](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-29); see Ref. [[13]](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-13).
*   [Binary BCH code](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/bch)—A family of BCH codes supports an infinite family of combinatorial 4-designs [[30](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-30),[31](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-31)].
*   [[48,24,12] self-dual code](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/self_dual_48_24_12)—Fixed-weight codewords of extremal self-dual doubly even codes whose length divides 24 form a combinatorial 5-design [[18]](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-18). There are several designs associated with this code [[32]](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-32).
*   [Higman-Sims graph-adjacency code](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/higman-sims_graph)—Codewords of weight 36 of the Higman-Sims graph-adjacency code form a 2-(100,36,525) design [[33; Remark 1.7]](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-33)
*   [Hoffman-Singleton cycle code](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/hoffman-singleton)—The incidence matrix of the Hoffman-Singleton graph can be converted into a 2-(50,14,13) design [[33; Prop. 1.1]](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-33).
*   [Preparata code](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/preparata)—Preparata codewords of each weight form a 3-design [[34; pg. 471]](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-34).
*   [Nordstrom-Robinson (NR) code](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/nordstrom_robinson)—NR codewords give 3-(16,6,4), 3-(16,8,3), and 3-(16,10,24) designs [[34; pg. 164]](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-34).
*   [Mixed code](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/mixed)—Combinatorial designs have been generalized to mixed alphabets [[35]](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-35).
*   [Editing code](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/insertion_deletion)—Perfect deletion correcting codes can be constructed using combinatorial design theory [[36](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-36),[37](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-37)].
*   [(12,4 6,6)4 Dodecacode](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/dodecacode)—There exists a 5-(12,6,3) design in the dodecacode, and a 3-(11,5,4) design in the shortened dodecacode [[38]](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-38).
*   [Cyclic linear q-ary code](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/q-ary_cyclic)—Two families of cyclic q-ary codes support an infinite family of combinatorial 3-designs [[39]](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-39). The supports of all fixed-weight codewords of a q-ary cyclic code support a combinatorial 1-design [[11; Corr. 5.2.4]](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-11). 
*   [Lexicographic code](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/lexicographic)—Some lexicodes yield Steiner systems [[40]](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-40).
*   [ZRM code](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/zrm)—The weight-four codewords of the binary image of the dual of ZRM(1,m) form a Steiner system that is identical to that formed by the weight-four codewords of an extended Hamming code [[41]](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-41).
*   [Pseudo Golay code](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/pseudo_golay)—Supports of codewords of any fixed symmetrized type of pseudo Golay codes form a 5-design [[42](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-42)–[44](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-44)].
*   [Extended quaternary Golay code](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/quaternary_golay)—Supports of codewords of any fixed symmetrized type of the extended quaternary Golay code form a 5-design [[42](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-42)–[44](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-44)].
*   [Perfect-tensor code](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/ame)—Combinatorial designs and d-uniform quantum states are related [[45](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-45)–[47](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-47)].
*   [EA combinatorial-design QLDPC code](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/ea_design_qldpc)—Combinatorial designs can be used to construct EA QLDPC codes [[48]](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-48).
*   [Jump code](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/jump)—Certain types of combinatorial designs can be used to obtain jump codes [[49](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-49)–[51](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-51)].

Combinatorial designs are designs on a space of fixed-weight binary strings (a.k.a. Johnson association scheme) [[52](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-52),[53](https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/c/combinatorial_design#citation-53)]. Subspace designs reduce to combinatorial designs for q=2.

SUMMARY:
A balanced incomplete block design or BIBD (usually called for short a block design) is a collection _B_ of _b_ subsets (called _blocks_) of a finite set _X_ of _v_ elements, such that any element of _X_ is contained in the same number _r_ of blocks, every block has the same number _k_ of elements, and each pair of distinct elements appear together in the same number λ of blocks. A t-wise balanced design (or _t_ BD) of type _t_−(_v_,K,_λ_) is a _v_-set _X_ together with a family of subsets of _X_ (called _blocks_) whose sizes are in the set K, such that every _t_-subset of distinct elements of _X_ is contained in exactly _λ_ blocks.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Symmetric arrangement of finite sets

**Combinatorial design theory** is the part of [combinatorial](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Combinatorics "Combinatorics")[mathematics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mathematics "Mathematics") that deals with the existence, construction and properties of [systems of finite sets](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Set_system "Set system") whose arrangements satisfy generalized concepts of _balance_ and/or _symmetry_. These concepts are not made precise so that a wide range of objects can be thought of as being under the same umbrella. At times this might involve the numerical sizes of set intersections as in [block designs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Block_design "Block design"), while at other times it could involve the spatial arrangement of entries in an array as in [sudoku grids](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sudoku "Sudoku").

Combinatorial design theory can be applied to the area of [design of experiments](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Design_of_experiments "Design of experiments"). Some of the basic theory of combinatorial designs originated in the statistician [Ronald Fisher](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ronald_Fisher "Ronald Fisher")'s work on the design of biological experiments. Modern applications are also found in a wide gamut of areas including [finite geometry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Finite_geometry "Finite geometry"), [tournament scheduling](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tournament "Tournament"), [lotteries](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lottery "Lottery"), [mathematical chemistry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mathematical_chemistry "Mathematical chemistry"), [mathematical biology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mathematical_biology "Mathematical biology"), [algorithm design and analysis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Algorithm_design "Algorithm design"), [networking](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Computer_network "Computer network"), [group testing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Group_testing "Group testing") and [cryptography](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cryptography "Cryptography").[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Combinatorial_design#cite_note-1)

Example
-------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Combinatorial_design&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Example")]

[![Image 4](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Fano_plane.svg/250px-Fano_plane.svg.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Fano_plane.svg)

The [Fano plane](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fano_plane "Fano plane")

Given a certain number _n_ of people, is it possible to assign them to sets so that each person is in at least one set, each pair of people is in exactly one set together, every two sets have exactly one person in common, and no set contains everyone, all but one person, or exactly one person? The answer depends on _n_.

This has a solution only if _n_ has the form _q_ 2 + _q_ + 1. It is less simple to prove that a solution exists if _q_ is a [prime power](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prime_power "Prime power"). It is conjectured that these are the _only_ solutions. It has been further shown that if a solution exists for _q_ congruent to 1 or 2 [mod](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Modulo_operation "Modulo operation") 4, then _q_ is a sum of two [square numbers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Square_numbers "Square numbers"). This last result, the [Bruck–Ryser theorem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bruck%E2%80%93Ryser_theorem "Bruck–Ryser theorem"), is proved by a combination of constructive methods based on [finite fields](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Finite_field "Finite field") and an application of [quadratic forms](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quadratic_form "Quadratic form").

When such a structure does exist, it is called a finite [projective plane](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Projective_plane "Projective plane"); thus showing how [finite geometry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Finite_geometry "Finite geometry") and combinatorics intersect. When _q_=2, the projective plane is called the [Fano plane](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fano_plane "Fano plane").

History
-------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Combinatorial_design&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Combinatorial designs date to antiquity, with the [Lo Shu Square](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lo_Shu_Square "Lo Shu Square") being an early [magic square](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Magic_square "Magic square"). One of the earliest datable application of combinatorial design is found in India in the book _Brhat Samhita_ by Varahamihira, written around 587 AD, for the purpose of making perfumes using 4 substances selected from 16 different substances using a magic square.[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Combinatorial_design#cite_note-Hayashi-2)

Combinatorial designs developed along with the general growth of [combinatorics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Combinatorics "Combinatorics") from the 18th century, for example with [Latin squares](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Latin_square "Latin square") in the 18th century and [Steiner systems](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Steiner_system "Steiner system") in the 19th century. Designs have also been popular in [recreational mathematics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Recreational_mathematics "Recreational mathematics"), such as [Kirkman's schoolgirl problem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kirkman%27s_schoolgirl_problem "Kirkman's schoolgirl problem") (1850), and in practical problems, such as the scheduling of [round-robin tournaments](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Round-robin_tournament "Round-robin tournament") (solution published 1880s). In the 20th century designs were applied to the [design of experiments](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Design_of_experiments "Design of experiments"), notably Latin squares, [finite geometry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Finite_geometry "Finite geometry"), and [association schemes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Association_scheme "Association scheme"), yielding the field of [algebraic statistics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Algebraic_statistics "Algebraic statistics").

Fundamental combinatorial designs
---------------------------------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Combinatorial_design&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Fundamental combinatorial designs")]

The classical core of the subject of combinatorial designs is built around [balanced incomplete block designs (BIBDs)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Block_design "Block design"), [Hadamard matrices and Hadamard designs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hadamard_matrix "Hadamard matrix"), [symmetric BIBDs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Block_design#Symmetric_2-designs_(SBIBDs) "Block design"), [Latin squares](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Latin_square "Latin square"), [resolvable BIBDs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Block_design#Resolvable_2-designs "Block design"), [difference sets](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Difference_set "Difference set"), and pairwise balanced designs (PBDs).[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Combinatorial_design#cite_note-3) Other combinatorial designs are related to or have been developed from the study of these fundamental ones.

*   A **balanced incomplete block design** or BIBD (usually called for short a [block design](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Block_design "Block design")) is a collection _**B**_ of _b_ subsets (called _blocks_) of a finite set _X_ of _v_ elements, such that any element of _X_ is contained in the same number _r_ of blocks, every block has the same number _k_ of elements, and each pair of distinct elements appear together in the same number λ of blocks. BIBDs are also known as _2-designs_ and are often denoted as 2-(_v_,_k_,λ) designs. As an example, when λ = 1 and _b_ = _v_, we have a [projective plane](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Projective_plane "Projective plane"): _X_ is the point set of the plane and the blocks are the lines.
*   A **symmetric balanced incomplete block design** or **[SBIBD](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Block_design#Symmetric_2-designs_(SBIBDs) "Block design")** is a BIBD in which _v_=_b_ (the number of points equals the number of blocks). They are the single most important and well studied subclass of BIBDs. Projective planes, biplanes and Hadamard 2-designs are all SBIBDs. They are of particular interest since they are the extremal examples of [Fisher's inequality](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fisher%27s_inequality "Fisher's inequality") (_b_ ≥ _v_).
*   A **[resolvable BIBD](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Block_design#Resolvable_2-designs "Block design")** is a BIBD whose blocks can be partitioned into sets (called _parallel classes_), each of which forms a partition of the point set of the BIBD. The set of parallel classes is called a _resolution_ of the design. A solution of the famous [15 schoolgirl problem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/15_schoolgirl_problem "15 schoolgirl problem") is a resolution of a BIBD with _v_=15, _k_=3 and λ=1.[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Combinatorial_design#cite_note-4)
*   A **[Latin rectangle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Latin_rectangle "Latin rectangle")** is an _r_×_n_[matrix](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Matrix_(mathematics) "Matrix (mathematics)") that has the numbers 1,2,3,...,_n_ as its entries (or any other set of _n_ distinct symbols) with no number occurring more than once in any row or column where _r_≤_n_. An _n_×_n_ Latin rectangle is called a [Latin square](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Latin_square "Latin square"). If _r_<_n_, then it is possible to append _n_−_r_ rows to an _r_×_n_ Latin rectangle to form a Latin square, using [Hall's marriage theorem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hall%27s_marriage_theorem "Hall's marriage theorem").[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Combinatorial_design#cite_note-5)

Two Latin squares of order _n_ are said to be _orthogonal_ if the set of all ordered pairs consisting of the corresponding entries in the two squares has _n_ 2 distinct members (all possible ordered pairs occur). A set of Latin squares of the same order forms a set of **[mutually orthogonal Latin squares (MOLS)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Orthogonal_Latin_squares "Orthogonal Latin squares")** if every pair of Latin squares in the set are orthogonal. There can be at most _n_−1 squares in a set of MOLS of order _n_. A set of _n_−1 MOLS of order _n_ can be used to construct a [projective plane](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Projective_plane "Projective plane") of order _n_ (and conversely).
*   A (_v_, _k_, λ) **[difference set](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Difference_set "Difference set")** is a [subset](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Subset "Subset")**D** of a [group](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Group_(mathematics) "Group (mathematics)")**G** such that the [order](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Order_of_a_group "Order of a group") of **G** is _v_, the [size](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cardinality "Cardinality") of **D** is _k_, and every nonidentity element of **G** can be expressed as a product _d_ 1 _d_ 2−1 of elements of **D** in exactly λ ways (when **G** is written with a multiplicative operation).[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Combinatorial_design#cite_note-6)

If **D** is a difference set, and _g_ in **G**, then _g_**D**={_gd_: _d_ in **D**} is also a difference set, and is called a **translate** of **D**. The set of all translates of a difference set **D** forms a [symmetric BIBD](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Block_design#Symmetric_2-designs_(SBIBDs) "Block design"). In such a design there are _v_ elements and _v_ blocks. Each block of the design consists of _k_ points, each point is contained in _k_ blocks. Any two blocks have exactly λ elements in common and any two points appear together in λ blocks. This SBIBD is called the _development_ of **D**.[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Combinatorial_design#cite_note-7)In particular, if λ = 1, then the difference set gives rise to a [projective plane](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Projective_plane "Projective plane"). An example of a (7,3,1) difference set in the group Z/7 Z{\displaystyle \mathbb {Z} /7\mathbb {Z} }![Image 5: {\displaystyle \mathbb {Z} /7\mathbb {Z} }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9e4256c2ea1e4ed73467f3af24355e364bed2c0f) (an abelian group written additively) is the subset {1,2,4}. The development of this difference set gives the [Fano plane](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fano_plane "Fano plane").Since every difference set gives an SBIBD, the parameter set must satisfy the [Bruck–Ryser–Chowla theorem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bruck%E2%80%93Ryser%E2%80%93Chowla_theorem "Bruck–Ryser–Chowla theorem"), but not every SBIBD gives a difference set.
*   An **[Hadamard matrix](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hadamard_matrix "Hadamard matrix")** of order _m_ is an _m_ × _m_ matrix **H** whose entries are ±1 such that **HH**⊤=_m_**I**_m_, where **H**⊤ is the transpose of **H** and **I**_m_ is the _m_×_m_ identity matrix. An Hadamard matrix can be put into _standardized form_ (that is, converted to an equivalent Hadamard matrix) where the first row and first column entries are all+1. If the order _m_>2 then _m_ must be a multiple of 4.

Given an Hadamard matrix of order 4 _a_ in standardized form, remove the first row and first column and convert every −1 to a 0. The resulting 0–1 matrix **M** is the [incidence matrix](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Incidence_matrix "Incidence matrix") of a symmetric 2−(4 _a_−1, 2 _a_−1, _a_−1) design called an **Hadamard 2-design**.[[8]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Combinatorial_design#cite_note-8) This construction is reversible, and the incidence matrix of a symmetric 2-design with these parameters can be used to form an Hadamard matrix of order 4 _a_. When _a_=2 we obtain the, by now familiar, [Fano plane](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fano_plane "Fano plane") as an Hadamard 2-design.
*    A **pairwise balanced design** (or PBD) is a set _X_ together with a family of subsets of _X_ (which need not have the same size and may contain repeats) such that every pair of distinct elements of _X_ is contained in exactly λ (a positive integer) subsets. The set _X_ is allowed to be one of the subsets, and if all the subsets are copies of _X_, the PBD is called _trivial_. The size of _X_ is _v_ and the number of subsets in the family (counted with multiplicity) is _b_.

[Fisher's inequality](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fisher%27s_inequality "Fisher's inequality") holds for PBDs:[[9]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Combinatorial_design#cite_note-9) For any non-trivial PBD, _v_≤_b_.This result also generalizes the famous [Erdős–De Bruijn theorem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/De_Bruijn%E2%80%93Erd%C5%91s_theorem_(incidence_geometry) "De Bruijn–Erdős theorem (incidence geometry)"): For a PBD with _λ_=1 having no blocks of size 1 or size _v_, _v_≤_b_, with equality if and only if the PBD is a [projective plane](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Projective_plane "Projective plane") or a near-pencil.[[10]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Combinatorial_design#cite_note-10)

Other combinatorial designs
---------------------------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Combinatorial_design&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Other combinatorial designs")]

The _Handbook of Combinatorial Designs_ ([Colbourn & Dinitz 2007](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Combinatorial_design#CITEREFColbournDinitz2007)) has, amongst others, 65 chapters, each devoted to a combinatorial design other than those given above. A partial listing is given below:

*   [Association schemes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Association_scheme "Association scheme")
*   A **balanced ternary design** BTD(_V_, _B_; _ρ_ 1, _ρ_ 2, _R_; _K_, Λ) is an arrangement of _V_ elements into _B_[multisets](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Multiset "Multiset") (blocks), each of cardinality _K_ (_K_ ≤ _V_), satisfying:

1.   Each element appears _R_ = _ρ_ 1 + 2 _ρ_ 2 times altogether, with multiplicity one in exactly _ρ_ 1 blocks and multiplicity two in exactly _ρ_ 2 blocks.
2.   Every pair of distinct elements appears Λ times (counted with multiplicity); that is, if _m_ _vb_ is the multiplicity of the element _v_ in block _b_, then for every pair of distinct elements _v_ and _w_, ∑b=1 B m v b m w b=Λ{\displaystyle \sum _{b=1}^{B}m_{vb}m_{wb}=\Lambda }![Image 6: {\displaystyle \sum _{b=1}^{B}m_{vb}m_{wb}=\Lambda }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/629164a83fd992bfd3d8bf89c956dab3f1acce53).

For example, one of the only two nonisomorphic BTD(4,8;2,3,8;4,6)s (blocks are columns) is:[[11]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Combinatorial_design#cite_note-11)
1 1 1 2 2 3 1 1
1 1 1 2 2 3 2 2
2 3 4 3 4 4 3 3
2 3 4 3 4 4 4 4
The [incidence matrix](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Incidence_matrix "Incidence matrix") of a BTD (where the entries are the multiplicities of the elements in the blocks) can be used to form a ternary error-correcting code analogous to the way binary codes are formed from the incidence matrices of BIBDs.[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Combinatorial_design#cite_note-12)
*   A **balanced tournament design** of order _n_ (a BTD(_n_)) is an arrangement of all the distinct unordered pairs of a 2 _n_-set _V_ into an _n_×(2 _n_−1) array such that

1.   every element of _V_ appears precisely once in each column, and
2.   every element of _V_ appears at most twice in each row.

An example of a BTD(3) is given by
1 6 3 5 2 3 4 5 2 4
2 5 4 6 1 4 1 3 3 6
3 4 1 2 5 6 2 6 1 5
The columns of a BTD(_n_) provide a [1-factorization](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1-factorization "1-factorization") of the complete graph on 2 _n_ vertices, _K_ 2 _n_.[[13]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Combinatorial_design#cite_note-13)BTD(_n_)s can be used to schedule [round-robin tournaments](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Round-robin_tournament "Round-robin tournament"): the rows represent the locations, the columns the rounds of play and the entries are the competing players or teams.
*   [Bent functions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bent_function "Bent function")
*   [Costas arrays](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Costas_array "Costas array")
*   [Factorial designs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Factorial_design "Factorial design")
*   A **frequency square** (**F**-square) is a higher order generalization of a [Latin square](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Latin_square "Latin square"). Let _S_ = {_s_ 1,_s_ 2, ..., _s_ _m_} be a set of distinct symbols and (_λ_ 1, _λ_ 2, ...,_λ_ _m_) a _frequency vector_ of positive integers. A _frequency square_ of order _n_ is an _n_ × _n_ array in which each symbol _s_ _i_ occurs _λ_ _i_ times, _i_ = 1,2,...,_m_, in each row and column. The _order_ _n_ = _λ_ 1+_λ_ 2+...+_λ_ _m_. An F-square is in _standard form_ if in the first row and column, all occurrences of _s_ _i_ precede those of _s_ _j_ whenever _i_<_j_.

A frequency square _F_ 1 of order _n_ based on the set {_s_ 1,_s_ 2, ..., _s_ _m_} with frequency vector (_λ_ 1, _λ_ 2, ...,_λ_ _m_) and a frequency square _F_ 2, also of order _n_, based on the set {_t_ 1,_t_ 2, ..., _t_ _k_} with frequency vector (_μ_ 1, _μ_ 2, ...,_μ_ _k_) are _orthogonal_ if every ordered pair (_s_ _i_, _t_ _j_) appears precisely _λ_ _i_ _μ_ _j_ times when _F_ 1 and _F_ 2 are superimposed.
*   **Hall triple systems** (HTSs) are [Steiner triple systems (STSs)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Steiner_system "Steiner system") (but the blocks are called _lines_) with the property that the substructure generated by two intersecting lines is isomorphic to the [finite affine plane](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Projective_plane#Affine_planes "Projective plane") AG(2,3).

Any affine space AG(_n_,3) gives an example of an HTS. Such an HTS is an _affine_ HTS. Nonaffine HTSs also exist.The number of points of an HTS is 3 m for some integer _m_≥2. Nonaffine HTSs exist for any _m_≥4 and do not exist for _m_=2 or 3.[[14]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Combinatorial_design#cite_note-14)Every Steiner triple system is equivalent to a Steiner [quasigroup](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quasigroup "Quasigroup") ([idempotent](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Idempotent "Idempotent"), [commutative](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Commutative "Commutative") and satisfying (_xy_)_y_=_x_ for all _x_ and _y_). A Hall triple system is equivalent to a Steiner quasigroup which is [distributive](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Distributive_property "Distributive property"), that is, satisfies _a_(_xy_) = (_ax_)(_ay_) for all _a_,_x_,_y_ in the quasigroup.[[15]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Combinatorial_design#cite_note-15)
*   Let _S_ be a set of 2 _n_ elements. A **Howell design**, H(_s_,2 _n_) (on symbol set _S_) is an _s_ × _s_ array such that:

1.   Each cell of the array is either empty or contains an unordered pair from _S_,
2.   Each symbol occurs exactly once in each row and column of the array, and
3.   Every unordered pair of symbols occurs in at most one cell of the array.

An example of an _H_(4,6) is
0 4 1 3 2 5
2 3 1 4 0 5
3 5 2 4 0 1
1 5 0 2 3 4
An H(2 _n_−1, 2 _n_) is a [Room square](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Room_square "Room square") of side 2 _n_−1, and thus the Howell designs generalize the concept of Room squares.The pairs of symbols in the cells of a Howell design

enriched_points.expanded_content: Combinatorial designs exhibit balance and symmetry, ensuring each element appears uniformly in subsets, crucial for constructing effective error-correcting codes.
These designs underlie various coding methods, including Reed-Muller codes, enhancing data integrity in communication systems, storage, and cryptographic applications.
Combinatorial codes demonstrate exceptional error correction capabilities, allowing for efficient decoding and greater resilience against data corruption in transmission.
Compared to traditional methods, combinatorial designs offer superior performance and flexibility in constructing codes, making them ideal for modern communication requirements.
